title: Extract Formam path and schema primitives into focused modules
goal: Reduce azq/formam/storage.py to a thinner Stage 2 facade by moving path ownership and canonical record normalization into smaller modules with clearer names.
context: The pre-Stage-3 refactor report identifies azq/formam/storage.py as the largest active Stage 2 hotspot and recommends decomposing it by concern before Agenda work begins.
Stage 2 is functionally complete, so Wave D should preserve behavior while improving module boundaries.
The implementation plan says each engine should stay small and filesystem-driven, which the current all-in-one storage module no longer satisfies.
requirements: Create a Formam paths module that owns canonical data/form/deliverables/ and data/form/maps/ path decisions and directory helpers.
Create a Formam schemas module that owns deliverable normalization, goal-map normalization, and schema constants that do not need to live in storage.py.
Update azq/formam/storage.py to import and re-export moved helpers if a stable public surface is needed.
Keep external Stage 2 behavior unchanged while shrinking the storage module's responsibility.
Do not introduce Agenda task concepts or new Formam command behavior in this task.
acceptance_criteria: Path helpers and schema normalization no longer live only inside azq/formam/storage.py.
The moved modules have names that match their responsibilities clearly.
Existing Stage 2 Formam behavior still works through the shared storage facade.
suggested_commit_message: extract formam path and schema primitives

title: Extract deliverable repository and markdown helpers from Formam storage
goal: Move deliverable-specific read, write, parse, and id-allocation behavior into a smaller dedicated module so deliverable persistence stops living inside a general grab-bag storage file.
context: The refactor report calls out deliverable serialization, repository reads, and next-id allocation as one of the major concerns currently packed into azq/formam/storage.py.
Stage 3 will need a clean deliverable boundary because Agenda tasks must descend from deliverables rather than bypassing them.
Wave D should preserve the canonical DELIV_### markdown format while making the owning module more obvious.
requirements: Create a dedicated deliverable storage module for deliverable markdown serialization, parsing, repository reads, exact-id lookup, goal-based lookup, and next-id allocation.
Update build.py and deliverables.py to depend on the extracted deliverable module through clear imports.
Leave azq/formam/storage.py as a narrow compatibility layer or facade rather than the primary home of deliverable-specific logic.
Preserve deterministic ordering and the canonical Stage 2 file format.
Do not broaden into map refactors in this task.
acceptance_criteria: Deliverable-specific persistence behavior lives in a module whose name matches that responsibility.
build.py and deliverables.py no longer need a wide grab-bag import from storage.py for deliverable operations.
Canonical deliverable reads, writes, and DELIV_### allocation remain unchanged in behavior.

title: Extract goal-map repository helpers and move dependency-edge derivation beside map logic
goal: Give goal-map persistence and dependency-edge derivation a clearer home so map behavior stops competing with unrelated storage concerns.
context: The pre-Stage-3 refactor report specifically recommends moving dependency-edge derivation out of azq/formam/storage.py and closer to map logic.
Stage 2 goal maps are visible structure artifacts, so the code that shapes them should be easy to find and reason about.
This refactor should keep maps human-readable and conservative rather than introducing richer planning behavior.
requirements: Create a dedicated goal-map storage module for map markdown serialization, parsing, repository reads, exact-id lookup, and canonical writes.
Move dependency-edge derivation out of azq/formam/storage.py and place it in azq/formam/maps.py or another clearly map-focused module.
Update map-building code to use the extracted helpers through narrow imports.
Preserve the canonical GOAL_<goal_id>_MAP.md format and the current sparse, inspectable Stage 2 map behavior.
Do not introduce Agenda DAGs, scheduling, or task generation.
acceptance_criteria: Goal-map persistence has a dedicated module with an intuitive name.
Dependency-edge derivation no longer lives inside the general storage facade.
The visible Stage 2 goal-map artifact behavior remains stable and human-readable.
suggested_commit_message: extract formam goal map storage helpers

title: Split azq/cli.py into thinner engine-specific command routers
goal: Keep azq/cli.py small and obvious by moving Scintilla, Finis, and Formam command dispatch into narrower engine-specific modules before Stage 3 adds Agenda commands.
context: The refactor report marks azq/cli.py as a high-priority split because it now mixes top-level help text, capture UX, Finis migration readiness, and command dispatch for multiple engines.
The implementation plan says the CLI should stay aligned with the craft stages and filesystem pipeline.
If Stage 3 lands on the current centralized dispatch file, the top-level CLI will sprawl again immediately.
requirements: Keep one thin main() in azq/cli.py that handles top-level dispatch and shared command listing.
Create engine-specific router modules for Scintilla, Finis, and Formam command handling.
Move the Finis readiness trigger to the narrowest reasonable place so goal-backed commands still migrate safely before use.
Preserve the current public Stage 2 command set and usage behavior.
Do not add any new Agenda commands in this task.
acceptance_criteria: azq/cli.py becomes a smaller top-level entrypoint rather than a full multi-engine router.
Engine-specific dispatch behavior lives in files whose names match their responsibility.
The existing Stage 2 command loop still works without user-visible broadening.
suggested_commit_message: split cli into engine specific routers

title: Rename the staged task runner to match its real scope and keep a thin compatibility path
goal: Correct the Stage-1-only naming drift in the Codex runner now that it drives multiple stages, while keeping operator scripts readable and stable.
context: The pre-Stage-3 refactor report identifies azq_codex_stage1_task_runner.py as both oversized and misnamed because it now handles Stage 1 and Stage 2 work.
This runner refactor is not new craft functionality, but it directly supports the staged engineering process described by the build-task documents.
Wave D is a reasonable place to fix naming drift before later stages add more ritual weight.
requirements: Introduce a stage-agnostic runner entrypoint such as azq_codex_task_runner.py.
Keep azq_codex_stage1_task_runner.py as a thin compatibility shim or redirect path so existing operator habits do not break abruptly.
Update the shell ritual and live runbooks to refer to the stage-agnostic runner name where appropriate.
Avoid changing task semantics or expanding runner behavior in this task.
Keep the result clearer and more intuitive than the current Stage-1-only filename.
acceptance_criteria: The primary runner filename no longer implies a false Stage-1-only scope.
Existing ritual entrypoints still work or fail over cleanly through a compatibility path.
Operator-facing references align with the renamed runner.
suggested_commit_message: rename codex runner to stage agnostic entrypoint

title: Add regression coverage for the Wave D refactor seams
goal: Prove that the Wave D decomposition changes preserved Stage 2 behavior by extending automated coverage around the public Formam and CLI entry surfaces that moved.
context: Wave D is intentionally a refactor wave, so its main risk is silent behavior drift rather than missing new features.
The Stage 2 regression suite already covers the live Formam baseline and is the right place to lock down the moved seams.
The implementation plan favors stable, inspectable filesystem behavior over clever internal rewrites.
requirements: Extend the Stage 2 regression suite to cover the public helpers or entrypoints affected by the Formam storage split.
Extend coverage so CLI routing changes still prove build, list, show, and map behavior remains intact.
Add at least one regression assertion that the stage-agnostic runner path remains invokable if the rename task is completed.
Keep the tests focused on preserved behavior rather than internal module layout.
Do not broaden this task into Stage 3 Agenda testing.
acceptance_criteria: The Stage 2 regression suite proves the Wave D refactors did not change the live Formam command behavior.
Moved module boundaries are protected by tests at the public surface level.
Wave D ends with Stage 2 behavior still demonstrably stable.
suggested_commit_message: add regression coverage for stage 2 wave d refactors
